title: Supreme Court Ruling Halts Louisiana Primaries, Throwing Midterm Map into Chaos
summary: Louisiana’s congressional primary, scheduled for May 16, has been abruptly suspended after the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that one of the state’s districts relied too heavily on race, violating the Voting Rights Act. Governor Jeff Landry and Attorney General Liz Murrill, both Republicans, announced Thursday that the ruling effectively blocks the state from proceeding under its current map. Early voting had been slated to start Saturday. “The State is currently enjoined from carrying out congressional elections under the current map,” they said in a joint statement, adding that they are working with the legislature to “develop a path forward.” The suspension drew sharp criticism from Democrats, who argue it disenfranchises voters. “This is going to cause mass confusion among voters — Democrats, Republicans, white, Black, everybody,” said state Senator Royce Duplessis, a New Orleans Democrat. “What they’re effectively doing is changing the rules of the game in the middle of the game. It’s rigging the system.” The ruling is the latest twist in a national redistricting battle that has seen both parties redraw House maps to gain an edge in the November midterms. Louisiana currently has four Republican and two Democratic representatives. A revised map could allow the GOP to pick up at least one more seat. The Supreme Court’s conservative majority found that Louisiana officials had prioritized race when creating a second majority-Black district, represented by Democrat Cleo Fields. The decision significantly weakens minority protections under the Voting Rights Act, following a pattern: last year, the court had required Alabama to create a second majority-Black district, prompting Louisiana to adopt its now-struck-down map. The chaos extends beyond Louisiana. On Wednesday, Florida adopted a new map backed by Governor Ron DeSantis that could net Republicans several seats. President Trump had previously urged Texas Republicans to redraw districts, and California Democrats responded in kind. With the primary on hold and no clear timeline, Louisiana voters face uncertainty — and the outcome could tip the balance of power in Congress.
why_it_matters: Redistricting determines who represents communities in Congress for the next decade, so a single court ruling can shift the political balance of the entire country. If the Voting Rights Act is weakened, minority groups may find it harder to elect candidates of their choice, undoing decades of civil rights progress.

keywords.explanation: ordered by a court to stop doing something

a landmark 1965 U.S. law aimed at stopping racial discrimination in voting
when the judges on a court lean toward conservative ideas, often favoring limited government and traditional values
the situation where two sides have roughly equal influence, so one small change can determine who controls a government body

options: The Supreme Court ruled a district relied too heavily on race
The governor canceled it due to low turnout
questions.correct_answer: The Supreme Court ruled a district relied too heavily on race

background_read: Every ten years, after the U.S. Census, states redraw their congressional districts to reflect population changes. This process, called redistricting, is often fiercely partisan because the way district lines are drawn can determine which party wins seats.

The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was designed to protect racial minorities from being packed into districts in ways that weakened their voting power. In recent years, the Supreme Court has issued mixed rulings: in 2023 it required Alabama to create a second majority-Black district, but its new conservative majority also signaled a retreat from aggressive federal oversight of districting.

Louisiana’s now-suspended primary is part of this broader tug-of-war. Governor Jeff Landry (a Republican) and Attorney General Liz Murrill are tasked with drawing a lawful replacement map, while Democrats and voting rights advocates warn of disenfranchisement. Royce Duplessis, a state senator from New Orleans, is among those arguing the court ruling throws an election into chaos right before early voting was scheduled to begin.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Louisiana’s congressional primary suspended after Supreme Court ruling on racial gerrymandering
├─ WHO: Governor Jeff Landry and Attorney General Liz Murrill announce suspension
└─ WHY: The Court found the current map violated the Voting Rights Act by prioritizing race
REACTION: Democrats decry confusion and voter disenfranchisement
└─ QUOTE: State Senator Royce Duplessis says it's 'changing the rules in the middle of the game'
├─ NUMBERS: Louisiana currently has 4 Republicans, 2 Democrats in the House
├─ BENEFIT: A new map could let the GOP gain at least one more seat
└─ PATTERN: Last year the Court required Alabama to add a second majority-Black district, but now weaker Voting Rights Act protection
├─ FLORIDA: Governor DeSantis backed a map that could net Republicans seats
├─ TEXAS: President Trump urged Republicans to redraw districts
UNRESOLVED: Timeline uncertain, voter uncertainty, outcome could tip balance of power in Congress

perspectives.description: Supporters of the ruling, like many conservative legal groups, argue that racial gerrymandering—even when intended to protect minority representation—can itself violate the principle of color-blind law. They see the decision as restoring a focus on geographic communities rather than racial balancing.

Voting rights advocates and many Democrats argue that the decision guts a key protection of the Voting Rights Act, making it easier for mapmakers to pack Black voters into a few districts and dilute their voting power. They warn that suspending a primary weeks before early voting disenfranchises millions and creates confusion that could depress turnout.

The redistricting battle reflects the high-stakes nature of gerrymandering: both parties have long drawn maps to maximize advantage. The Supreme Court has increasingly left such fights to state legislatures, with ambiguous guidance that fuels constant litigation. Louisiana’s crisis is a textbook case of how a federal court’s shifting interpretation can upend entire election cycles overnight.

With the midterms on the horizon, Louisiana must quickly produce a new map that satisfies the Voting Rights Act, or the election will be further delayed. The outcome could either embolden Republicans to push more favorable maps in other states or trigger a backlash that leads to independent redistricting commissions to reduce partisan meddling.
